summary: French New Wave director and critic. Best known for Celine and Julie Go Boating (1974) — two women repeatedly explore a mysterious haunted house in Paris, emerging each time with a hard candy in their mouths. Also directed the 13-hour Out 1 (inspired by Balzac's Human Comedy), L'amour fou, and La Belle Noiseuse (based on Balzac's 'The Unknown Masterpiece'). As a critic at Cahiers du Cinéma, Rivette wrote a famous essay condemning Gillo Pontecorvo's use of tracking shots in Kapo.

works.name: Celine and Julie Go Boating (Céline et Julie vont en bateau, 1974)
works.description: Phantasmagorical film in which two women — Celine (Juliet Berto) and Julie (Dominique Labourier) — repeatedly enter a mysterious haunted house at 7 bis, rue du Nadir-aux-Pommes, each time emerging with no memory of what happened and a hard candy in their mouths. They suck on the candies to recall the events. Opens with one title character dropping her scarf in a Parisian park like the White Rabbit from Alice in Wonderland; the other chases her (including onto a tram) for 15 minutes. Juliet Berto and Dominique Labourier moved in together during production and recalled their dreams to each other to get into character. Also references 'The House of Fiction.' Wikipedia

works.name: Out 1 (1971)
works.description: 13-hour epic film (full title: Out 1: Noli me tangere). First publicly shown in the U.S. at the Museum of the Moving Image in 2006, 35 years after its first showing. Inspired by Balzac's Human Comedy. The first episode is titled 'From Lili to Thomas.' Uses 16mm footage for the TV crew shooting a theater company, and 35mm film for the crew filming the footage — Rivette's first film with extensive improvisation. A color film using B&W stills and a B&W segment from the previous episode to connect episodes. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical / Critical Work
works.description: French New Wave director and critic at Cahiers du Cinéma. Famous essay criticizing Gillo Pontecorvo's Kapo for 'tilting the camera up' at Emmanuelle Riva's dying hand, stating the filmmaker 'deserves nothing but contempt.' The quote is often misattributed to Godard. Led a putsch against Eric Rohmer to become head editor of Cahiers du Cinéma, then took it in a Marxist direction. Wrote 'The Genius of Howard Hawks.' Also called Showgirls 'one of the great American films of the last few years.' Le Pont du Nord features a dragon sculpture in Paris's Parc de la Villette.

clues.clue: Famous Cahiers essay condemning Gillo Pontecorvo's Kapo — criticized the tracking shot that 'tilts the camera up' to Emmanuelle Riva's dying hand; called it deserving 'nothing but contempt'
examples: This critic wrote that he had the 'most profound contempt' for the man who tilted the camera up to view Emmanuelle Riva's hand as her character dies against a barbed wire fence in the film Kapo
Jacques Rivette, in his criticism of Gillo Pontecorvo's Kapo, wrote that the man who used this technique [tracking shot] 'deserves nothing but contempt'

clues.clue: Frequently misattributed quote linking camera movements to morality was actually by Luc Moullet, cited by Rivette
examples: A quote often misattributed to Godard linking camera movements to morality was actually said by Luc Moullet and used by this director to criticize Gillo Pontecorvo's Kapo

comprehensive_summary:
Jacques Rivette (1928–2016) was a French New Wave director and film critic associated with Cahiers du Cinéma. As a critic, he is famous for a 1961 essay condemning Gillo Pontecorvo's film Kapo, in which he wrote that he had the 'most profound contempt' for any filmmaker who would tilt the camera up to linger on Emmanuelle Riva's hand as her character dies against a barbed wire fence — a critique of the aestheticization of atrocity. A quote linking camera movements to morality is often misattributed to Godard but was actually by Luc Moullet, cited by Rivette. He also led a putsch against Eric Rohmer to become head editor of Cahiers du Cinéma, subsequently taking it in a Marxist direction, and wrote 'The Genius of Howard Hawks.' He infamously called Showgirls 'one of the great American films of the last few years.'

Rivette's most celebrated film is Celine and Julie Go Boating (Céline et Julie vont en bateau, 1974). Two women — Celine (Juliet Berto) and Julie (Dominique Labourier) — repeatedly visit a mysterious haunted house at 7 bis, rue du Nadir-aux-Pommes, each time leaving with no memory of what happened inside and a hard candy mysteriously in their mouths. They suck on the candies to recapture their memories of what occurred. The film opens with Julie dropping her scarf in a Parisian park, like the White Rabbit from Alice in Wonderland, and Celine chasing her through Paris (including onto a tram) for 15 minutes. The two actresses moved in together during production and recalled their dreams to each other to prepare for their roles. The film alludes to a theater called 'The House of Fiction.'

Out 1 (1971, full title: Out 1: Noli me tangere) is a 13-hour film inspired by Balzac's Human Comedy, whose first episode is titled 'From Lili to Thomas.' It used 16mm film for the TV crew footage of a theater company and 35mm film for the crew themselves — Rivette's first film with extensive improvisation. It was first publicly shown in the U.S. at the Museum of the Moving Image in 2006, 35 years after its premiere. L'amour fou (1969) depicts the breakdown of the relationship between Sébastien, who directs rehearsals of Racine's Andromaque, and his wife Claire (playing Hermione), who is overwhelmed by the presence of a TV crew filming the rehearsals. La Belle Noiseuse (1991) is a four-hour film based on Balzac's 'The Unknown Masterpiece' from the Human Comedy — the story of the master painter Frenhofer who destroys his unfinished masterpiece. Le Pont du Nord (1982) features a dragon sculpture in Paris's Parc de la Villette.
